proposals.title: Microanalytic Coding of Parent-Child Synchrony: A Novel Window into the Neurobehavioral Mechanisms of Attachment-Based Intervention Efficacy
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on attachment-based interventions often relies on global behavioral ratings or parent-report measures, which may lack the granularity to capture subtle, moment-to-moment changes in dyadic interaction that are theorized to be the active ingredients of change. This gap limits our understanding of how interventions precisely reshape the neurobehavioral underpinnings of the attachment system, a core theme in van IJzendoorn's work. Integrating microanalytic methods with pre-existing neuroimaging and genetic data from large cohorts (like ENIGMA) offers a powerful, multi-level approach to studying developmental mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a video-feedback intervention designed to enhance parental sensitivity will lead to quantifiable increases in micro-level behavioral synchrony (e.g., coordinated gaze, vocal turn-taking, affective mirroring) during free-play, and that these micro-analytic gains will mediate improvements in child attachment security and correlate with pre-intervention neural correlates of temperament and genetic susceptibility markers.
proposals.method: We will apply frame-by-frame microanalytic coding (e.g., using Datavyu or ELAN software) to existing pre- and post-intervention video recordings of parent-child free-play from a cohort study. Coding will focus on objective, low-inference behaviors such as mutual gaze, responsive touch, and vocal reciprocity within 1-second epochs. These micro-coded data will be analyzed using multilevel time-series models (e.g., Actor-Partner Interdependence Models) to quantify synchrony. The derived synchrony metrics will then be integrated as mediators in structural equation models testing intervention outcomes and as correlates in analyses of pre-existing child structural MRI data (e.g., amygdala-prefrontal cortex connectivity) and polygenic susceptibility scores.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide an objective, fine-grained behavioral assay for evaluating the efficacy of attachment interventions, moving beyond summary scores. It will directly test the mechanism of change (behavioral synchrony) posited by attachment theory. Furthermore, by linking micro-behavior to brain structure and genetic susceptibility, it will offer a novel multi-level explanation for differential intervention response, bridging developmental science with neurogenetics.
proposals.novelty: While microanalysis is established in developmental science, its application as a primary outcome and mediating mechanism in a genetically-informed, neurodevelopmentally-grounded intervention study for attachment is novel. This approach uniquely operationalizes the 'positive interaction' process at a granular level to explain differential susceptibility outcomes in a way that global ratings cannot.

proposals.title: Dynamic Interplay of Parenting Stress and Child Behavior in Daily Life: An Ecological Momentary Assessment Study of Differential Susceptibility
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including van IJzendoorn's work, has established that childhood experiences shape development 'for better and for worse,' but relies heavily on retrospective or lab-based measures. The real-world, moment-to-day dynamics of parenting stress and child behavior, which are crucial for understanding the mechanisms of differential susceptibility, remain a black box. Capturing these micro-level fluctuations is essential for moving from static associations to dynamic, ecologically valid models of parent-child interaction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that children with genetic or temperamental markers of higher susceptibility (e.g., polygenic scores for environmental sensitivity, inhibited temperament) will exhibit stronger within-day associations between fluctuations in maternal parenting stress and fluctuations in their own observed emotional and behavioral states, demonstrating differential susceptibility in real time.
proposals.method: We will recruit 200 mother-child dyads (children aged 3-5 years) with pre-existing genetic and temperament data from a cohort study. For 14 consecutive days, mothers will receive 5 random smartphone prompts daily via a custom EMA app (e.g., using Ethica Data or similar) to report current stress, affect, and child behavior. Concurrently, children will wear a lightweight accelerometer to objectively capture activity levels. Data will be analyzed using multilevel vector autoregressive (mlVAR) modeling to map temporal, bidirectional associations, and multilevel moderation analysis will test if susceptibility markers moderate the stress-behavior link.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first fine-grained, real-time evidence for the differential susceptibility hypothesis in naturalistic settings, linking genetic and neural susceptibility markers (from prior work) to dynamic behavioral outcomes. It will advance the field by identifying specific, modifiable moments of risk and opportunity in daily parenting, directly informing the development of just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs) to support families.
proposals.novelty: While EMA has been used in mental health research, its application to test core developmental theories like differential susceptibility by integrating real-time behavioral data with pre-existing neural (e.g., ENIGMA-based brain structure) and genetic susceptibility markers in a community sample of young children is novel and methodologically innovative.

proposals.title: Dyadic Physiological Co-regulation as a Biomarker of Attachment-Based Intervention Efficacy: A Randomized Controlled Trial
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 attachment research has robustly linked sensitive caregiving to child socio-emotional outcomes, yet intervention studies often rely on behavioral observations or self-reports, which can be subjective. Concurrently, developmental neuroscience has established that parent-child physiological synchrony, particularly in heart rate variability (HRV), is a marker of co-regulation and attachment security. Integrating these psychophysiological measures into intervention science offers a novel, objective biomarker to quantify intervention impact on the dyadic system.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an attachment-based parenting intervention will significantly increase dyadic physiological co-regulation (indexed by parent-child HRV synchrony during a stress-eliciting task) compared to a control group, and that this increase will mediate improvements in observed child attachment security and parental sensitivity.
proposals.method: In a randomized controlled trial, 150 parent-child dyads (children aged 2-4 years) will be assigned to a video-feedback intervention (VIPP-SD) or a waitlist control. Pre- and post-intervention, dyads will undergo the Strange Situation Procedure (SSP) while wearing lightweight electrocardiogram monitors to record inter-beat intervals. We will compute time-domain (RMSSD) and frequency-domain (HF-HRV) indices of child and parent HRV. Dyadic co-regulation will be quantified using cross-correlation and windowed cross-lagged correlation analyses to assess bidirectional synchrony. Machine learning models (e.g., Random Forest classifiers) will be trained to predict intervention group membership from pre-post changes in the multi-modal feature set (HRV synchrony metrics, coded SSP behaviors, and parent-reported temperament).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first direct tests of whether a behavioral intervention can systematically alter a dyadic psychophysiological process. It will contribute a novel, objective biomarker (HRV co-regulation) to the toolkit for evaluating early childhood interventions. Furthermore, by applying predictive modeling, it will identify which combination of physiological and behavioral measures best captures intervention-induced change, advancing a more precise science of prevention.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has associated physiological synchrony with attachment, no pre-2026 RCT has explicitly targeted it as a mechanism of change in a proven intervention. The integration of predictive machine learning on a combined physiological-behavioral dataset to model intervention impact moves beyond traditional regression approaches, offering a new methodological paradigm for developmental psychopathology.

proposals.title: Uncovering the Neurobiological and Attachment Signatures of Familial Resilience: A Multi-Modal Study of High-Risk, High-Functioning Dyads
proposals.motivation: Research on childhood adversity has predominantly focused on psychopathology, creating a deficit model. A critical inverse problem is identifying why some families remain resilient despite high cumulative risk. Grounded in pre-2026 differential susceptibility theory and neuroimaging advances, this study shifts focus to positive outcomes to identify naturally occurring protective factors, which can inform targeted, strengths-based interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that resilient families, characterized by the absence of child behavior problems despite high environmental risk, will show a distinct profile combining secure attachment patterns, positive parenting behaviors, and specific structural brain characteristics (e.g., in prefrontal and limbic regions) that buffer against stress and promote adaptive functioning.
proposals.method: We will conduct a nested case-control study within a large, pre-existing population-based cohort (e.g., Generation R). Using propensity score matching, we will identify a 'resilient' group (high risk, no behavior problems) and matched 'vulnerable' (high risk, with problems) and 'low-risk' groups. Multi-modal assessment will include the Strange Situation Procedure (attachment), observed positive parent-child interactions, and structural MRI (T1-weighted) for children. We will employ machine learning (specifically, Random Forest and Support Vector Machine algorithms) to identify the key combination of attachment, behavioral, and neuroanatomical predictors that classify resilient status, controlling for genetic susceptibility via polygenic risk scores for mental health.
proposals.contribution: This study will move the field from risk to resilience by empirically defining a multi-systemic resilience phenotype. It will directly inform the development of preventive interventions by highlighting modifiable protective factors (e.g., specific positive interaction patterns). Furthermore, it will test the neurobiological correlates of differential susceptibility in a real-world, high-risk context.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its explicit 'inverse problem' design, actively seeking resilient cases within high-risk cohorts, and in its integrative multi-modal methodology combining gold-standard attachment assessment with neuroimaging and predictive machine learning to move beyond association to classification.

proposals.title: Beyond Acceptance: Non-Verbal IQ as a Differential Susceptibility Factor for the Quality of Peer Relationships in Middle Childhood
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on peer relationships often focuses on acceptance or rejection, with findings suggesting non-verbal IQ is irrelevant. This narrow focus overlooks the nuanced quality of relationships, a critical factor for socio-emotional development. Grounded in van IJzendoorn's work on differential susceptibility, this study investigates whether cognitive ability acts as a 'for-better-and-for-worse' factor, shaping the depth and emotional tone of peer interactions, not just their existence.
proposals.hypothesis: Non-verbal IQ moderates the association between a child's social environment (e.g., classroom climate) and the observed quality of their dyadic peer relationships. Higher IQ will be associated with higher-quality relationships in supportive contexts but lower-quality relationships in adverse contexts, consistent with a differential susceptibility model.
proposals.method: We will use a multi-cohort design, integrating data from population-based cohorts (e.g., Generation R). Peer relationship quality will be assessed via teacher-reported closeness/conflict and observed dyadic interaction paradigms coded for reciprocity and affective tone. We will employ moderated regression and latent growth curve modeling within a structural equation framework to test IQ-by-environment interactions. Machine learning techniques (e.g., Random Forest with SHAP values) will be used for exploratory analysis to identify non-linear patterns and key predictors of relationship quality beyond IQ.
proposals.contribution: This study will shift the focus from peer status to relationship quality, offering a more nuanced understanding of social development. It will provide empirical evidence for cognitive ability as a plasticity factor, extending the differential susceptibility framework into the domain of peer relationships. The findings can inform targeted interventions that consider a child's cognitive profile to foster positive peer experiences.
proposals.novelty: It challenges the null finding on IQ by proposing it is not a main effect but a moderator of environmental influence on relationship quality, a mechanism not previously tested. It integrates the differential susceptibility model—central to the researcher's recent work on psychosis—with observational social development research.

proposals.title: Agent-Based Modeling of Social Group Dynamics: Simulating the Impact of Autistic Traits on Peer Rejection and Positive Affiliation in Childhood
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on autism and social development often focuses on individual deficits, with less understanding of how autistic traits dynamically shape peer group formation and rejection. This is a critical developmental issue, as peer rejection in childhood is a robust predictor of later psychopathology. Computational social science offers new tools to model these complex, emergent social processes, bridging individual-level traits with group-level outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in simulated social environments, a higher prevalence of agent traits modeling social communication differences and preference for predictable interaction will lead to emergent patterns of increased peer rejection and segregated group formation, but that these dynamics can be moderated by environmental factors promoting shared, structured positive activities.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model (ABM) using the NetLogo platform. Agents will be endowed with behavioral rules derived from pre-2026 empirical data on social motivation, communication style, and behavioral rigidity associated with autistic traits. The model will simulate repeated interaction rounds in a virtual schoolyard. Key algorithms will include a similarity-attraction rule for affiliation and a stochastic rejection rule influenced by trait mismatch and group norms. We will systematically vary population-level trait distributions and environmental scaffolds (e.g., presence of structured cooperative tasks) to test for differential susceptibility effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a formal, mechanistic framework for understanding how individual neurodevelopmental differences translate into systemic social outcomes like rejection. It will identify potential environmental levers (positive, structured experiences) that could mitigate rejection, aligning with van IJzendoorn's interest in differential susceptibility. The work will generate testable, dynamic hypotheses for future longitudinal and intervention studies in developmental psychology.
proposals.novelty: While ABMs have been used in sociology, their application to model the social impact of neurodevelopmental traits in childhood is novel. This approach moves beyond static correlation to simulate the *process* of rejection, integrating van IJzendoorn's expertise in differential susceptibility with computational methods to study 'for better and for worse' outcomes.

proposals.title: A Cross-Cohort Mega-Analysis of Differential Susceptibility: Testing Gene-Environment Interactions Linking Positive Childhood Experiences to Brain Development and Resilience
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on childhood adversity and brain development is extensive, but the neurobiological mechanisms of positive experiences and differential susceptibility remain understudied at a population level. Large-scale, harmonized analyses across diverse cohorts are needed to move beyond single-cohort findings and establish robust, generalizable models of how genetic factors moderate the impact of positive environments on brain structure and later mental health outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that polygenic scores for neuroplasticity and stress reactivity will moderate the association between positive childhood experiences (e.g., secure attachment, supportive parenting) and structural brain development (e.g., amygdala, prefrontal cortex volumes), such that genetically susceptible children will show both more favorable brain outcomes in positive contexts and poorer outcomes in adverse contexts, compared to less susceptible children.
proposals.method: We will conduct a coordinated individual-participant-data meta-analysis across 4-6 large international birth cohorts (e.g., Generation R, ALSPAC, ABCD, Norwegian Mother, Father and Child Cohort Study) with pre-existing genetic, longitudinal environmental, and structural neuroimaging (MRI) data. Using a two-stage approach, we will first perform within-cohort analyses applying linear mixed-effects models to test Gene x Environment (GxE) interactions on brain phenotypes. Subsequently, fixed-effects meta-analysis will pool estimates. We will employ latent profile analysis to define positive experience composites and use pre-registered, hypothesis-driven regions of interest based on the ENIGMA consortium protocols.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, cross-national evidence for neurobiological differential susceptibility, directly testing the "for better and for worse" model. It will generate a harmonized analytical framework for future multi-cohort GxE research and identify specific brain circuits most sensitive to positive environmental influences, informing targeted early interventions.
proposals.novelty: While multi-cohort mega-analyses exist for main effects of adversity, applying this powerful design to test GxE interactions for *positive* experiences in relation to brain structure is novel. It shifts the focus from risk to susceptibility and resilience mechanisms at a population neuroimaging scale.

proposals.title: Differential Susceptibility in Daily Life: A Micro-Longitudinal Investigation of Attachment Security, Peer Interactions, and Cortisol Reactivity in Young Children
proposals.motivation: While large-scale studies link early attachment to later social outcomes, the proximal, within-child mechanisms remain a 'black box.' Pre-2026 research on differential susceptibility suggests genetic factors may modulate sensitivity to both positive and negative social environments, but this has rarely been tested in real-time, naturalistic peer contexts during early childhood, a critical period for social-emotional development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that children with higher polygenic susceptibility scores (e.g., based on dopamine-related genes) will show stronger day-to-day associations between the quality of peer interactions and their physiological stress response (cortisol), but only if they have an insecure, not a secure, attachment history, demonstrating a 'for better and for worse' pattern in daily micro-processes.
proposals.method: We will conduct intensive, micro-longitudinal case studies of N=30 children (aged 4-5), assessed 5 times daily for 10 consecutive school days. Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) via caregiver/parent reports will capture peer interaction quality. Salivary cortisol samples will be collected at each EMA prompt. Pre-existing Strange Situation Procedure (SSP) classifications and polygenic susceptibility scores (from prior cohort data) will serve as moderators. Analysis will use multilevel time-lagged models and vector autoregression (VAR) to examine within-child dynamics and bidirectional effects.
proposals.contribution: This will provide unprecedented granular data on how attachment and genetic susceptibility shape real-time socio-biological responses, moving beyond between-person correlations. It will directly test differential susceptibility theory at the micro-temporal scale and generate mechanistic hypotheses for how daily experiences 'get under the skin' to influence developmental trajectories.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of micro-longitudinal physiology with established attachment and genetic frameworks in ecologically valid peer settings represents a significant methodological and conceptual scaling down from population-level studies to the intensive within-child processes theorized to drive development.

proposals.title: Differential Susceptibility in the Social Sphere: A Multi-Informant Study of Genetic Sensitivity, Parenting, and Adult-Reported Child Peer Relationships
proposals.motivation: Peer relationships in childhood are crucial for socio-emotional development and long-term mental health, yet they are often assessed through limited perspectives. Pre-2026 research, including van IJzendoorn's work on differential susceptibility, highlights how genetic factors (e.g., polygenic scores for dopamine regulation) can moderate the effect of parenting on child outcomes, but this framework is rarely applied to positive social outcomes like peer competence. Integrating multi-informant reports (teachers, parents) provides a more ecologically valid measure of this key developmental domain.
proposals.hypothesis: Children with higher polygenic susceptibility scores will show stronger associations between observed positive parenting quality and better adult-reported peer relationship outcomes, demonstrating 'for better' effects in a social context, compared to children with lower genetic susceptibility.
proposals.method: We will utilize data from a large, pre-existing longitudinal cohort (e.g., Generation R, ALSPAC) with genetic data, observed parenting measures (e.g., Etch-A-Sketch task), and adult-reported child peer relationships (e.g., Teacher Report of Social Adjustment, Parent Peer Relationship Scale). A polygenic susceptibility score (PSS) will be constructed from dopamine-related genes (e.g., DRD4, DRD2, DAT1) based on pre-2026 differential susceptibility literature. The analysis will employ moderated regression models and simple slopes analysis to test the Gene x Environment interaction, controlling for child temperament, gender, and socioeconomic status.
proposals.contribution: This study will extend the differential susceptibility model into the understudied domain of positive peer relationships, moving beyond psychopathology. It will validate a multi-informant approach to measuring peer competence as a key positive outcome. The findings can inform targeted interventions, suggesting that genetically susceptible children may benefit disproportionately from parenting programs that foster social skills.
proposals.novelty: While differential susceptibility has been tested for externalizing/internalizing problems, its application to normative, positive social outcomes using a multi-informant design is novel. Combining observed parenting with genetic susceptibility to predict teacher and parent reports of peer relationships integrates developmental psychology and genetics in a unique way.

proposals.title: Neural Plasticity of Attachment Security: A Longitudinal fMRI Study of Brain Changes During the Video-feedback Intervention to promote Positive Parenting and Sensitive Discipline (VIPP-SD)
proposals.motivation: Learning-based attachment interventions like VIPP-SD are effective in enhancing parental sensitivity and child attachment security, but their neural mechanisms of change remain a 'black box'. Understanding the brain-level plasticity associated with successful intervention is crucial for refining theories of environmental susceptibility and optimizing treatments. This bridges the gap between behavioral attachment research and developmental neuroscience, addressing a key pre-2026 knowledge gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that successful VIPP-SD intervention, leading to improved parental sensitivity and child attachment security, will be associated with measurable changes in brain structure and function within the parental caregiving network (e.g., anterior cingulate cortex, insula, amygdala) and the child's social-emotional processing circuitry.
proposals.method: We propose a longitudinal, randomized controlled trial with 100 parent-child dyads (children aged 12-36 months) at risk for insecure attachment. Dyads will be randomized to VIPP-SD or a control intervention. Structural (T1-weighted, diffusion tensor imaging) and functional MRI (during a child face-viewing and a parenting stress task) will be collected from parents and (where feasible, using natural sleep protocols) children at pre-intervention, post-intervention, and 6-month follow-up. Primary analyses will use voxel-based morphometry (FSL-VBM) for grey matter, tract-based spatial statistics (TBSS) for white matter, and general linear modeling (FSL FEAT) for task-fMRI, with intervention group as a between-subjects factor and time as a within-subjects factor.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct evidence of neuroplasticity in the adult caregiving brain following a behavioral attachment intervention and link it to child outcomes. It will identify neural biomarkers of intervention success and resilience, moving the field beyond correlational brain-behavior studies. The findings will inform a neurobiological model of how positive environmental change alters attachment-related circuits.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has associated brain structure with childhood experiences or temperament, no pre-2026 study has used a randomized design to track intervention-induced brain changes in both members of the dyad. This shifts the focus from static neural correlates to dynamic neural change processes underlying attachment security.

proposals.title: Shaping Secure Attachment: A Proof-of-Concept Trial Applying Operant Techniques from Autism Therapy to Foster Parent-Infant Synchrony
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research shows that insecure attachment is a significant risk factor for later socio-emotional problems, yet interventions are often broad and not precisely targeted at the micro-behaviors of dyadic interaction. Concurrently, Applied Behavior Analysis (ABA) for autism has developed highly precise operant techniques for shaping complex social behaviors, but these have not been systematically adapted to the attachment context. This project addresses a critical gap by exploring whether the precision of behavioral shaping can be harnessed to build the foundational behaviors of secure attachment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured intervention using discrete-trial training and chaining procedures, adapted from ABA, will effectively increase the frequency and quality of secure-base and safe-haven behaviors in infants at risk for insecure attachment, as measured by standardized observational coding and parent report.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized controlled pilot with 60 caregiver-infant dyads (infants aged 12-18 months with elevated social withdrawal scores). The intervention group will receive 10 weekly sessions where parents are coached to use prompting, differential reinforcement, and chaining to shape specific infant attachment behaviors (e.g., proximity-seeking, effective distress signaling). Fidelity will be monitored via video coding using the Noldus Observer XT. Primary outcomes will be the Strange Situation Procedure (SSP) classification and the Attachment Q-Sort. We will use linear mixed models to analyze pre-post changes in behavioral frequencies and SSP security scores.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical test of applying core ABA operant techniques to attachment formation. It will generate a novel, highly granular intervention protocol targeting specific attachment behaviors. If effective, it could offer a complementary, skill-based approach to existing attachment therapies, particularly for high-risk dyads who may not respond to less structured interventions.
proposals.novelty: While ABA principles are well-established, their direct application to the dyadic, emotion-laden process of attachment building is novel and non-obvious, representing a unique synthesis of behavioral and developmental-clinical frameworks. It moves beyond correlational 'predictor' models to test a causal, mechanistic model of how specific caregiver responses shape attachment behaviors.

proposals.title: A Virtual Reality Paradigm for Assessing Differential Susceptibility to Attachment Security Priming in Middle Childhood: A Pilot fMRI Study
proposals.motivation: Middle childhood is a critical period for the consolidation of internal working models of attachment, yet experimental paradigms to study attachment-related fears and their neural correlates are limited. Pre-2026 research by van IJzendoorn and others highlights the importance of positive experiences and differential susceptibility, but lacks scalable, controlled methods to induce and measure attachment-relevant emotional states in real-time. Virtual reality (VR) offers a novel, ecologically valid tool to create standardized, replicable exposure scenarios that can be synchronized with neuroimaging.
proposals.hypothesis: Children with a history of insecure attachment, as measured by pre-existing questionnaires, will show heightened amygdala reactivity to VR-based attachment threat scenarios (e.g., virtual separation), but will also demonstrate a greater positive neural and behavioral response (e.g., increased prefrontal cortex engagement and self-reported security) following a VR-based attachment security priming scenario, supporting a differential susceptibility model.
proposals.method: We will develop a within-subjects VR-fMRI paradigm for children aged 8-10. Using the Unity game engine, we will create two counterbalanced scenarios: a 'threat' scenario (e.g., getting lost in a virtual crowd) and a 'security priming' scenario (e.g., a virtual caregiver providing comfort). Scenarios will be presented via an MRI-compatible headset (e.g., NordicNeuroLab). Neural responses will be measured using a 3T fMRI scanner. Analyses will employ a general linear model (GLM) in SPM12 or FSL, contrasting brain activation (amygdala, ventral striatum, vmPFC) during threat vs. security conditions, moderated by attachment security scores from the Security Scale.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first validated VR-fMRI protocol for experimentally manipulating attachment-related states in middle childhood. It will directly test the differential susceptibility hypothesis in the attachment domain by linking genetic or temperamental susceptibility markers (e.g., polygenic scores or inhibited temperament) to neural plasticity in response to positive VR exposure. The results will inform the development of scalable, positive intervention tools.
proposals.novelty: While VR has been used for phobia exposure, its application to experimentally probe the neural mechanisms of attachment security and differential susceptibility in typically developing children is novel. The integration of a positive exposure (security priming) within a differential susceptibility framework, combined with multimodal brain-behavior assessment, moves beyond prior correlational or observational attachment studies.

proposals.title: Differential Susceptibility in Fatherhood: A Longitudinal Study of Hormonal Plasticity, Paternal Behavior, and Child Socio-Emotional Development from Pregnancy to Middle Childhood
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on the biology of parenting has focused heavily on mothers and the postpartum period in infancy, leaving a significant gap in understanding the longitudinal trajectories of paternal hormonal changes and their behavioral consequences. This is crucial because paternal caregiving is increasingly recognized as a key determinant of child development, and the differential susceptibility framework suggests fathers may vary in their neuroendocrine sensitivity to the caregiving context, with implications for child outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fathers exhibiting greater longitudinal plasticity in key hormones (testosterone, oxytocin, cortisol) from the prenatal period through middle childhood will demonstrate more sensitive and engaged paternal behavior, which in turn will predict more positive child socio-emotional outcomes, particularly for children with a genetic susceptibility profile (e.g., DRD4, 5-HTTLPR).
proposals.method: We propose a longitudinal cohort study of 300 first-time father-child dyads, assessing hormones via salivary assays at 7 timepoints from pregnancy to child age 8. Paternal behavior will be coded from structured videotaped interactions (e.g., free play, challenge task) using the Emotional Availability Scales. Child socio-emotional development will be measured via parent/teacher reports and behavioral tasks. We will employ Latent Growth Curve Modeling to map hormonal trajectories and use Structural Equation Modeling with moderated mediation to test if paternal behavior mediates the hormone-child outcome link, and if this pathway is moderated by child polygenic susceptibility scores derived from pre-2026 GWA studies.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated, longitudinal map of paternal neuroendocrine-behavioral dynamics beyond infancy, directly testing the differential susceptibility model in fatherhood. It will advance attachment and developmental neuroscience by identifying specific hormonal patterns that underpin positive paternal caregiving and buffer or amplify genetic susceptibility in children, offering targets for early intervention.
proposals.novelty: This extends van IJzendoorn's work on genetic susceptibility by applying it to *paternal* hormonal plasticity across a much longer developmental window, moving beyond the typical focus on maternal or infant cortisol to a multi-hormone, father-child systems approach.

proposals.title: Paternal Sensitivity and Infant Attachment Security: A Cross-Cultural Replication Study Testing the Generalizability of the Father-Child Attachment Model
proposals.motivation: The majority of attachment research, including foundational work on father-child relationships, has been conducted in Western, educated, industrialized, rich, and democratic (WEIRD) contexts. This limits our understanding of the universality versus cultural specificity of attachment processes. Replicating core findings on paternal sensitivity and infant attachment with diverse fathers is crucial for validating developmental theories and informing culturally sensitive family interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the positive association between observed paternal sensitivity and infant-father attachment security, as established in Western samples, will be replicated across diverse cultural and socioeconomic groups, but that the strength of this association will be moderated by specific cultural norms regarding fatherhood and family structure.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-site, cross-cultural study recruiting father-infant dyads (N=600, infants aged 12-18 months) from six distinct contexts varying in collectivism/individualism, socioeconomic status, and family structure (e.g., nuclear vs. extended households). Paternal sensitivity will be coded from standardized video-recorded play interactions using the Ainsworth Sensitivity Scales. Infant-father attachment security will be assessed using the Strange Situation Procedure. We will employ multi-group confirmatory factor analysis to test measurement invariance of the constructs, followed by multi-level regression and moderation analysis using the PROCESS macro in SPSS to test the main and moderated effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, direct test of the generalizability of the paternal sensitivity-attachment link. It will advance attachment theory by delineating its cultural boundaries and generate a unique dataset on normative fathering in understudied populations. Findings will directly inform the development of parenting programs that are effective across diverse family contexts.
proposals.novelty: While cross-cultural comparisons in maternal attachment exist, a focused, hypothesis-driven replication of the father-child attachment model across deliberately sampled diverse contexts is novel. It moves beyond documenting mean differences to testing the universality of a core theoretical mechanism.

proposals.title: Father-Infant Behavioral and Neural Synchrony: A Micro-Coding and fNIRS Hyperscanning Study on the Role of Paternal Sensitivity
proposals.motivation: While maternal attachment is well-studied, the unique contribution of father-infant interactions to child development, particularly through mechanisms of behavioral synchrony, remains less understood. Pre-2026 research highlights the importance of positive childhood experiences and differential susceptibility, yet lacks fine-grained, objective analysis of real-time father-infant dyadic coordination and its potential brain-to-brain coupling correlates.
proposals.hypothesis: Higher levels of observed paternal sensitivity during free-play will predict stronger dyadic behavioral synchrony, which in turn will be associated with increased neural synchrony (inter-brain connectivity) in prefrontal regions involved in social regulation, as measured via functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS) hyperscanning.
proposals.method: We will recruit 100 father-infant (6-12 months) dyads. Father sensitivity and infant responsiveness will be micro-coded second-by-second from a 10-minute free-play session using the Coding Interactive Behavior (CIB) manual. Behavioral synchrony will be quantified using cross-recurrence quantification analysis (CRQA), a nonlinear dynamical systems method that captures temporal coordination. Concurrently, neural activity will be recorded using dual-head fNIRS hyperscanning, focusing on the prefrontal cortex. Inter-brain synchrony will be calculated using wavelet transform coherence (WTC) on the hemodynamic signals.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, multi-method framework for quantifying father-infant dyadic processes, linking observed behavior to underlying neural mechanisms. It will advance attachment theory by empirically testing the role of paternal care in co-regulatory synchrony and offer a model for integrating micro-analytic social psychology methods with developmental neuroscience.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely applies the established concept of behavioral synchrony and the emerging technique of fNIRS hyperscanning specifically to the understudied father-infant dyad, moving beyond maternal focus to test a dual-brain model of paternal attachment.

proposals.title: Fatherhood Beyond the WEIRD: A Cross-Cultural Study of Paternal Caregiving, Oxytocin, and Child Attachment Security
proposals.motivation: Research on the hormonal and neural substrates of fatherhood and child attachment is overwhelmingly based on Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, and Democratic (WEIRD) societies. This limits our understanding of how paternal caregiving and its biological correlates manifest under diverse cultural models of fatherhood, such as those in small-scale, non-Western societies where paternal roles and daily involvement can differ substantially. Testing the universality versus cultural specificity of these biobehavioral associations is crucial for a complete theory of human attachment and parenting.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the positive association between paternal oxytocin levels, sensitive caregiving behavior, and child attachment security, observed in WEIRD populations, will be moderated by the cultural salience and expectations of the father's role, with stronger associations in societies where direct, nurturing paternal care is a central cultural norm.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-site field study in two small-scale societies with contrasting fatherhood models (e.g., high-engagement Aka foragers vs. more distant Tsimane horticulturalists), plus a WEIRD comparison group. Using a longitudinal design, we will collect salivary oxytocin from fathers pre- and post-interaction with their infants. Caregiving sensitivity will be coded from standardized videotaped interactions (e.g., Still-Face Paradigm). Infant attachment security will be assessed using the Attachment Q-Sort. We will employ multi-group structural equation modeling (SEM) in Mplus to test for measurement invariance and to compare the strength of the oxytocin-caregiving-attachment pathways across cultural groups.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct test of the cultural moderation of biobehavioral synchrony in father-infant dyads. It will move the field beyond WEIRD-centric models by delineating which aspects of the paternal caregiving system are human universals and which are culturally shaped. The findings will critically inform theories of differential susceptibility and the evolutionary basis of fatherhood.
proposals.novelty: While cross-cultural attachment research exists, no study has yet integrated direct hormonal assessment of fathers with behavioral attachment measures across culturally divergent, small-scale societies to explicitly test for moderation of biobehavioral pathways.

proposals.title: Deconstructing the Binary: A Multi-Method Investigation of Social Role Mediation in Gender Differences in Child-Parent Attachment Security
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 attachment research often treats gender as a binary, biologically-rooted explanatory variable for differences in attachment security and parenting sensitivity, potentially conflating biological sex with socially constructed roles. This approach obscures the mechanisms behind observed differences and limits the generalizability of findings. A more nuanced understanding is critical for developing effective, equitable family interventions and moving beyond essentialist explanations in developmental science.
proposals.hypothesis: Observed gender differences in child attachment security and caregiver sensitivity are not primarily driven by biological sex, but are mediated by socially constructed parental roles (e.g., primary vs. secondary caregiver, gendered caregiving behaviors) and associated psychosocial factors (e.g., parental self-efficacy, work-family balance).
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of large, pre-existing longitudinal cohort data (e.g., Generation R, NICHD SECCYD) using a multi-method approach. First, we will employ causal mediation analysis within a Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) framework to test if social role variables (caregiving hours, role identity, gendered behavior scales) mediate the association between child/caregiver sex and attachment outcomes (Strange Situation, AQS). Second, we will use machine learning techniques, specifically Random Forest regression with SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values, to identify the key predictors of attachment security from a broad set of variables including biological, social, and psychological factors, assessing the relative importance of sex versus social role constructs.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, formal test of social role mediation in gender-attachment associations, shifting the theoretical focus from essentialist to contextual explanations. It will offer a novel analytical model for decomposing the variance attributed to sex and social factors, informing more precise and inclusive attachment theory. The findings will have direct implications for parenting programs and policies aimed at reducing gender-based disparities in caregiving outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have noted gender differences, this proposal uniquely applies formal causal mediation and predictive machine learning models from the researcher's recent work to explicitly test social constructionist hypotheses against biological default explanations within attachment science. It moves beyond correlation to model the specific pathways through which social roles might operate.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Normative Baseline Bias: A Multi-Cohort Re-Analysis of Attachment and Temperament Data to Establish Culturally Contextualized Developmental Norms
proposals.motivation: Developmental science has long relied on a universal, often North American, baseline for defining normative attachment and temperament, potentially pathologizing adaptive behaviors in other cultural contexts. This practice, critiqued theoretically, lacks empirical quantification of its scientific costs, such as biased effect sizes and misapplied diagnostic thresholds. Grounded in pre-2026 calls for incorporating positive experiences and cross-cultural sensitivity, this project aims to move from critique to concrete measurement.
proposals.hypothesis: Re-analyzing major developmental datasets using multiple culturally situated comparison groups will reveal systematic biases, including inflated effect sizes for minority groups and skewed diagnostic thresholds, when compared to analyses using a single universal baseline. The magnitude of this bias will be empirically quantifiable and vary by construct (e.g., inhibition, disorganization).
proposals.method: We will conduct a coordinated secondary analysis of large-scale, pre-2026 datasets (e.g., Generation R, ABCD, relevant ENIGMA-Anxiety cohorts) containing attachment (Strange Situation, AQS) and temperament (inhibited temperament) measures. Using multi-group confirmatory factor analysis and measurement invariance testing, we will establish culturally contextualized baselines. We will then apply causal inference techniques, specifically propensity score matching and targeted maximum likelihood estimation (TMLE), to quantify bias by comparing effect estimates of childhood predictors (e.g., maternal sensitivity) on outcomes (e.g., brain structure, obesity risk) derived from the universal versus contextualized baselines.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first empirical benchmark of the normative baseline bias's magnitude in key developmental domains. It will yield an open-source analytical framework and a preliminary set of validated, culturally contextualized comparison norms for attachment and temperament, offering researchers practical tools to mitigate bias. The work directly extends van IJzendoorn's focus on differential susceptibility by testing how cultural context moderates the 'for better and for worse' equation.
proposals.novelty: While cultural critique is established, this project innovates by applying causal inference and mega-analytic methods from pre-2026 neuroimaging consortia (ENIGMA) to empirically quantify the bias, transforming a theoretical debate into a measurable problem with concrete solutions. It integrates the researcher's expertise in attachment, positive outcomes, and brain-behavior studies within a novel methodological framework.

proposals.title: Beyond Trait Stability: A Multi-Wave, Multi-Context Investigation of State-Dependent Fluctuations in Infant Attachment Classifications
proposals.motivation: Attachment theory has long conceptualized classifications (secure, insecure) as relatively stable traits shaped by early caregiving. However, this assumption may oversimplify reality, potentially obscuring how contextual factors (e.g., parental stress, infant state) contribute to moment-to-moment variability in attachment behaviors. Testing this assumption is crucial for refining theory and for interventions, as targeting malleable state factors may be more effective than targeting presumed fixed traits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of the variance in attachment classifications is state-like and context-dependent, with observable fluctuations across repeated assessments within the same infant, particularly in response to variations in caregiver sensitivity and infant physiological state.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-wave longitudinal study with N=200 infant-caregiver dyads, assessing attachment via the Strange Situation Procedure (SSP) at 12, 15, and 18 months. At each wave, we will also measure concurrent contextual moderators: coded caregiver sensitivity during home visits, infant cortisol reactivity, and parental reported stress. To model stability and change, we will use Latent Transition Analysis (LTA) to estimate probabilities of classification change across waves. Multinomial logistic regression with cluster-robust standard errors will test if contextual variables predict these transitions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first rigorous, quantitative estimate of state-dependent fluctuation in attachment classifications, moving the field from a binary trait/state debate to a nuanced model. It will identify specific contextual 'levers' (e.g., parental stress) that predict shifts, offering novel targets for real-time, ecological momentary interventions aimed at promoting secure-base behavior.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined stability, no study has explicitly modeled attachment classifications as latent states with quantifiable transition probabilities linked to multi-method, concurrently assessed contextual triggers, integrating behavioral observation with physiological and psychosocial measures in a repeated-measures design.

proposals.title: Resolving Disorder-Specific Attachment Profiles: A Dimensional 'Spectroscopy' Analysis of Anxiety and Avoidance in Psychopathology
proposals.motivation: Research on attachment in psychopathology often yields mixed findings, with clinical groups showing heterogeneous profiles that are poorly captured by categorical secure/insecure classifications. This obscures the specific role of attachment dimensions (anxiety, avoidance) in disorder etiology and maintenance. A novel analytical framework is needed to clarify these complex, composite profiles and link them to clinical outcomes, building on the researcher's prior work on differential susceptibility and brain-behavior associations.
proposals.hypothesis: Clinical populations (e.g., OCD, psychosis-spectrum disorders) exhibit distinct, disorder-specific combinations of attachment anxiety and avoidance dimensions, which can be reliably mapped as unique 'spectral signatures' that predict symptom severity and treatment-relevant correlates better than traditional categorical models.
proposals.method: We will apply a spectroscopy-inspired multivariate framework to existing large-scale cohort data (e.g., Generation R, ABCD) and clinical samples. First, we will establish reference dimensional signatures for core patterns using latent profile analysis on attachment measures (e.g., ECR-R). Then, we will employ supervised machine learning (e.g., Random Forest, Support Vector Machines) and canonical correlation analysis to decompose the attachment profiles of clinical subgroups, identifying the salient dimensional weights that characterize each disorder. Model performance will be validated via cross-validation and tested for association with brain structure (using ENIGMA-style protocols) and longitudinal outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, dimensional mapping of attachment in psychopathology, moving beyond binary classifications to reveal precise, clinically meaningful profiles. It will generate testable models of how specific attachment dimension combinations confer risk for different disorders, directly informing targeted intervention strategies. The work will also establish a new analytical paradigm for studying psychological constructs as composite spectra.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct conceptual adaptation of spectroscopy to resolve psychological constructs, and in its application of multivariate decomposition techniques specifically to attachment dimensions to uncover disorder-specific signatures, a method not previously applied in this domain.

proposals.title: Attachment at the Edge: A Multi-Method Study of Stress, Resilience, and Relational Coping in High-Risk Non-Clinical Families
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on developmental psychopathology often dichotomizes populations into clinical and low-risk groups, potentially missing critical insights from high-risk individuals who remain resilient. This gap limits our understanding of the dynamic processes that tip development toward pathology or health. An ecological 'edge habitat' framework offers a novel lens to study these transitional, high-risk states as unique sources of information on vulnerability and adaptive capacity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that high-risk non-clinical families ('edge habitat') will exhibit distinct, heterogeneous profiles of attachment security, stress physiology, and relational coping that differ systematically from both low-risk stable families ('core habitat') and families with diagnosed attachment-related disorders ('degraded habitat').
proposals.method: We will recruit three groups (N=75 each): 1) High-risk non-clinical (e.g., low SES, parental psychopathology history but no child diagnosis), 2) Low-risk stable, and 3) Clinical (childhood anxiety/attachment disorders). Multi-method assessment includes the Strange Situation Procedure or Adult Attachment Interview, caregiver-reported attachment questionnaires, diurnal cortisol and cortisol reactivity to a mild stressor, and coded observations of parent-child relational coping during a problem-solving task. Data will be analyzed using latent profile analysis to identify distinct multi-system profiles within and across groups, followed by regression and ANOVA to test group differences and predictive models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a nuanced, process-oriented map of attachment functioning in an understudied population, identifying potential early bio-behavioral markers of risk and resilience. It will advance the application of differential susceptibility theory by empirically testing how positive and negative experiences manifest in a high-risk 'edge' context. Findings could inform more targeted, preventative interventions.
proposals.novelty: The conceptual novelty lies in explicitly adapting the ecological 'edge habitat' model to developmental psychology, framing high-risk non-clinical status as an informative transitional zone rather than a mere intermediate category. Methodologically, it integrates attachment assessment, stress physiology, and behavioral observation in a targeted three-group design to dissect mechanisms of resilience.

proposals.title: Attachment Security as a Differential Susceptibility Factor: A Multi-Modal Study of High-Risk, Non-Clinical, and Clinical Adolescents
proposals.motivation: Attachment research has traditionally focused on low-risk and clinical populations, creating a gap in understanding the developmental pathways of high-risk but non-clinical youth. These individuals, exposed to significant adversity (e.g., poverty, parental psychopathology) yet showing resilience, represent a critical group for testing differential susceptibility and positive adaptation models. Investigating their attachment profiles and neural correlates can illuminate protective mechanisms and inform targeted, strengths-based prevention strategies before clinical onset.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adolescents in the high-risk non-clinical group will exhibit a distinct pattern of attachment security and associated brain structure (e.g., in amygdala, prefrontal cortex, anterior cingulate) that is intermediate between low-risk and clinical groups, and that attachment security will moderate the link between early adversity and positive psychosocial outcomes specifically in this high-risk group.
proposals.method: We will recruit three well-characterized groups (N=75 each): low-risk controls, high-risk non-clinical (based on cumulative risk indices), and clinical adolescents with internalizing disorders. Attachment will be assessed using the Adult Attachment Interview (AAI), coded for security and dismissing/preoccupied states of mind. Structural MRI data (T1-weighted) will be analyzed using the ENIGMA consortium protocols for volumetric and cortical thickness analysis. We will employ a multi-step analytical approach: 1) MANCOVA to test for group differences in attachment and brain regions, 2) Latent Profile Analysis (LPA) to identify data-driven subgroups across measures, and 3) Moderated regression using the PROCESS macro to test if attachment security buffers the adversity-outcome link in the high-risk group.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated, multi-modal characterization of attachment in a resilient, high-risk adolescent sample, directly testing differential susceptibility theory. It will identify specific neural and psychological markers of positive adaptation under adversity, moving beyond a deficit-focused model. The findings will contribute to refining early identification and intervention by highlighting protective factors that can be fostered in at-risk youth.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, multi-method comparison of high-risk non-clinical youth to both clinical and low-risk groups, shifting the focus from psychopathology to resilience within an attachment framework. Integrating AAI-based attachment assessment with ENIGMA-standardized neuroimaging in this specific population is a novel approach to understanding the brain-behavior correlates of differential susceptibility.

proposals.title: Mapping the Interplay: A Network Analysis of Attachment Dimensions and Co-occurring Internalizing and Externalizing Symptoms in Childhood
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on childhood adversity and psychopathology, including Dr. van IJzendoorn's work on differential susceptibility, often treats attachment as a predictor of distinct symptom clusters. However, clinical reality shows high co-occurrence (comorbidity) of symptoms, which traditional linear models struggle to explain. Network analysis offers a novel framework to model these symptoms as a dynamic system, but its application to understand how specific attachment dimensions (security, anxiety, avoidance) relate to this symptom network remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that insecure attachment dimensions (anxiety and avoidance) will function as distinct, central nodes in a network of childhood clinical symptoms, with attachment anxiety most strongly connected to internalizing symptoms (e.g., anxiety, depression) and attachment avoidance bridging internalizing and externalizing symptoms (e.g., aggression, ADHD features).
proposals.method: We will employ Gaussian Graphical Model (GGM) network analysis with graphical LASSO regularization and EBIC model selection on existing longitudinal cohort data (e.g., Generation R). Nodes will include continuous measures of attachment (using the Security Scale or AQS), internalizing symptoms (CBCL/SCARED), and externalizing symptoms (CBCL/SDQ). We will estimate two networks (e.g., at ages 6 and 10) to assess stability, compute node centrality (strength, expected influence), and use the NetworkComparisonTest to examine gender differences.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond correlational models to identify which attachment dimensions are most influential in the symptom network, potentially revealing specific pathways to comorbidity. It will provide a mechanistic, system-level model that aligns with the RDoC framework and could inform more targeted interventions that address central attachment-related drivers of symptom co-occurrence.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis is applied to psychopathology, its integration with nuanced attachment theory dimensions (beyond secure/insecure categories) to explain childhood symptom co-occurrence is novel. This approach directly tests if attachment constructs act as keystones in the symptom network, a hypothesis not addressable with standard regression.

proposals.title: Caregiving Experience, Not Biological Parenthood, Drives Enhanced Neural Sensitivity to Child Faces: An ERP Investigation of the N170 Component
proposals.motivation: The N170 event-related potential (ERP) component, a neural marker of face processing, is reportedly enhanced in parents viewing child faces, suggesting a unique neurocognitive adaptation. However, pre-2026 research has often confounded biological parenthood with direct, intensive caregiving experience. This study aims to disentangle these factors, testing a core tenet of attachment theory—that caregiving behavioral systems are shaped by experience—at the neurophysiological level.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the amplitude of the N170 response to child faces will be significantly greater in individuals with substantial, hands-on caregiving experience (regardless of biological parenthood) compared to non-caregiving biological parents and non-parents, with no significant difference between the latter two groups.
proposals.method: We will recruit three matched groups: 1) primary caregiver parents, 2) biological parents with minimal caregiving involvement, and 3) non-parents with no caregiving experience. EEG will be recorded while participants complete a standardized oddball task presenting adult and child faces. The N170 component will be extracted from occipito-temporal electrodes (P7, P8, PO9, PO10). Statistical analysis will employ a mixed-design ANOVA (Group x Face Type), followed by planned contrasts. We will use robust, pre-2026 processing pipelines (e.g., EEGLAB/ERPLAB) for artifact rejection and component quantification.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide critical evidence for the plasticity of the adult caregiving network, demonstrating that neural tuning to child stimuli is a function of experiential learning. It will refine models of the 'parental brain' by shifting focus from a biological to a caregiving status. The findings could inform interventions aimed at supporting sensitive caregiving in diverse family structures, including adoptive and foster parents.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies associate N170 differences with parenthood, none have systematically dissociated the effects of biological status from direct, daily caregiving responsibility. This design directly tests the differential susceptibility hypothesis in the neurocognitive domain, asking whether caregiving experience is the primary driver of neural change.

proposals.title: The Dual Arousal Hypothesis of Parental Neural Processing: Disentangling Positive Motivation and Stress in the Own-Child Late Positive Potential (LPP)
proposals.motivation: Parental neural responses, particularly the enhanced Late Positive Potential (LPP) to own-child stimuli, are widely interpreted as a marker of positive motivation and caregiving engagement. However, this interpretation may be incomplete, as the LPP is a general index of motivated attention and arousal, which can also be elicited by negative or stressful stimuli. Given the established links between parenting stress, attachment insecurity, and child outcomes, it is critical to determine whether the 'own-child LPP' reflects a uniformly positive motivational state or a composite of positive engagement and stress-related arousal.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the enhanced LPP to own-child faces is not a unitary positive signal but reflects a dual-process mechanism, where its amplitude is positively associated with both self-reported positive caregiving motivation and with physiological (e.g., cortisol) and self-reported indices of parenting stress and negative arousal.
proposals.method: We will recruit 150 parent-child dyads (children aged 3-5 years) with varied attachment security (Strange Situation) and parental sensitivity (observed interactions). During EEG recording, parents will view images of their own child, an unfamiliar child, and standardized emotional faces. The LPP (300-1000ms post-stimulus at parietal-occipital sites) will be quantified. Concurrently, we will collect salivary cortisol samples pre- and post-task as a stress biomarker, and administer questionnaires on parenting stress, daily hassles, and positive caregiving feelings. We will use structural equation modeling (SEM) with latent variables to test the distinct contributions of positive motivation and stress indices to the own-child LPP amplitude, controlling for child temperament and parent gender.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a more nuanced, dual-process neurobiological model of parental brain function, moving beyond a purely positive interpretation. It will directly inform theories of differential susceptibility in parenting by identifying neural markers that may reflect vulnerability to stress alongside motivational strength. The findings could refine interventions by suggesting that reducing parenting stress may alter the neural processing of child cues without diminishing positive engagement.
proposals.novelty: While prior work associates the own-child LPP with positive outcomes, this proposal explicitly tests the counterintuitive hypothesis that a larger LPP also indexes parental stress, integrating neural, endocrine, and behavioral measures within a differential susceptibility framework to explain heterogeneity in parental brain responses.

proposals.title: Neural Synchrony as a Mechanism of Differential Susceptibility: A Dual-EEG Hyperscanning Study of Parent-Child Positive Interaction and Attachment Security
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including van IJzendoorn's work, highlights that genetic susceptibility moderates how childhood experiences shape development 'for better and for worse,' but the underlying neural mechanisms during live, positive social interactions remain opaque. Simultaneously, structural brain studies link early temperament to neural architecture, yet lack the temporal resolution to capture dynamic, interpersonal brain-to-brain coupling that may be foundational for attachment and positive outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that parent-child neural synchrony, measured via inter-brain phase-locking of event-related potentials (ERPs) during a structured positive interaction task, will be stronger in dyads with a secure attachment relationship and will moderate the association between child genetic susceptibility markers (e.g., DRD4, 5-HTTLPR) and observed positive behavioral outcomes.
proposals.method: We will employ dual-EEG hyperscanning (e.g., using two synchronized 64-channel systems) to record brain activity from parent-child dyads (N=100, children aged 4-6) during a live, video-mediated interaction paradigm involving shared positive affect and cooperative play. Neural synchrony will be quantified using pre-2026 established algorithms: wavelet transform coherence (WTC) and cross-correlation of ERP components (specifically, the N170/P300 complex) time-locked to moments of mutual gaze and shared joy. These neural metrics will be analyzed in conjunction with coded attachment behavior (Strange Situation), genetic data, and longitudinal measures of child socio-emotional adjustment.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct evidence linking real-time, inter-brain synchrony to the differential susceptibility framework, moving beyond structural correlates to a dynamic, dyadic neural mechanism. It will advance attachment theory by identifying a potential neural signature of the attachment bond during positive engagement and offer a novel biomarker for predicting which susceptible children benefit most from positive caregiving.
proposals.novelty: While hyperscanning has been used in adult studies, its integration with ERP analysis to dissect moment-by-moment neural coupling during naturalistic parent-child positive interactions is novel. Combining this with genetic susceptibility factors directly tests a neurobiological model of differential susceptibility in a live social context, a significant leap beyond correlational behavioral or single-brain studies.

proposals.title: Micro-Longitudinal Dynamics of Attachment Security: ERP Responses to a Controlled Parenting Stressor in Infants
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on the neurobiology of attachment has largely relied on cross-sectional or long-term longitudinal designs, missing the rapid, moment-to-moment dynamics of the infant brain's response to caregiver stress. Understanding these micro-processes is crucial for elucidating the mechanisms through which early positive or negative caregiving experiences shape neurodevelopment and differential susceptibility. This study bridges attachment theory with developmental neuroscience by capturing real-time neural plasticity during a stressor.
proposals.hypothesis: Infants with secure attachment patterns, as measured by the Strange Situation Procedure, will show a more rapid and pronounced recovery in the amplitude of the Negative Central (Nc) ERP component following a controlled parenting stressor (e.g., the Still-Face Paradigm), compared to infants with insecure attachment patterns.
proposals.method: We will employ a micro-longitudinal design with high-density EEG. Infants (N=60, 12-14 months) will undergo the Strange Situation to classify attachment security. In a subsequent lab visit, EEG will be recorded during a modified Still-Face Paradigm (baseline, stressor, reunion). Event-Related Potentials (ERPs) will be extracted in response to standard auditory probes presented repeatedly at 30-second intervals throughout the protocol. The Nc component, a marker of attentional allocation to salient stimuli, will be analyzed. Change in Nc amplitude will be modeled using multilevel growth curve analysis (e.g., using the nlme package in R) to test for differential recovery trajectories by attachment classification.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first fine-grained evidence of how attachment security modulates rapid neurocognitive stress recovery in infancy. It will move the field beyond structural brain correlates to dynamic, functional processes, directly testing the 'differential susceptibility' model at a neural level. Findings will offer a novel biomarker for resilience and vulnerability to early social stress.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the micro-longitudinal, repeated-measures ERP approach within a single experimental session to capture neural change, a significant shift from the static 'snapshot' comparisons dominant in pre-2026 developmental cognitive neuroscience. It uniquely links a well-validated behavioral stressor with second-by-second neural data to model dynamic recovery.

proposals.title: Beyond the Strange Situation: A Multi-Method Test of Attachment Measures in Detecting Differential Susceptibility to Parenting
proposals.motivation: The Strange Situation (SS) is the gold standard for infant attachment classification, but its status as the optimal measure for detecting gene-environment (GxE) interactions, particularly differential susceptibility, is an assumption. Pre-2026 research, including van IJzendoorn's work on positive outcomes, suggests that susceptibility markers (e.g., DRD4, 5-HTTLPR) might be more sensitively captured by other behavioral or physiological measures. Testing this assumption is crucial for refining models of how parenting influences child development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that alternative, less resource-intensive measures of infant social-emotional reactivity (e.g., observed positive affect, cortisol reactivity, heart rate variability during social challenges) will outperform the SS in detecting differential susceptibility effects, showing stronger interactions with parenting quality in predicting both positive and negative behavioral outcomes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of existing longitudinal cohorts with multi-method infant data (e.g., Generation R). Using a machine learning approach, we will train and compare predictive models. The outcome variables will be mid-childhood internalizing/externalizing problems and social competence. Predictors will include genetic susceptibility indices, parenting quality (observations), and their interactions with (a) SS classification and (b) continuous measures of infant positive/negative reactivity and physiological regulation. We will use Elastic Net regression for feature selection and model comparison, followed by hierarchical linear modeling to test the significance of identified GxE interactions, comparing effect sizes and variance explained across measurement modalities.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical evidence on the comparative validity of attachment measures for GxE research. It will advance the differential susceptibility framework by identifying more precise, scalable behavioral biomarkers. The findings could shift methodological practices in developmental psychopathology towards more efficient, continuous measures of infant susceptibility.
proposals.novelty: While the SS's validity for attachment is established, directly testing its supremacy for detecting susceptibility effects is novel. Combining a multi-method design with a predictive modeling framework to formally compare measures in this specific context moves beyond traditional correlational analyses.

proposals.title: Timing Matters: A Longitudinal Investigation of the Temporal Dynamics Between Environmental Sensitivity and Attachment Security from Infancy to Mid-Childhood
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on differential susceptibility and attachment often treats sensitivity and attachment as static traits, measuring them concurrently or with inconsistent lags. This obscures understanding of their dynamic interplay. The field lacks systematic evidence on how the time elapsed between these measurements influences the observed association, which is critical for interpreting longitudinal studies and designing effective, timely interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the strength of association between early environmental sensitivity (as a marker of differential susceptibility) and later attachment security is not constant but follows a non-linear function of the measurement interval, with optimal predictive windows in early development. Shorter and excessively long intervals will show attenuated effects.
proposals.method: We will conduct secondary data analysis on large, pre-existing longitudinal cohorts (e.g., Generation R, ABCD) with repeated measures of infant/childhood temperamental sensitivity (e.g., IBQ, CBQ) and attachment security (Strange Situation Procedure, Attachment Q-Sort, self-report). Using multilevel meta-analytic techniques and time-lagged structural equation modeling, we will model the association strength as a function of measurement interval. We will employ fractional polynomial regression and kernel-smoothing algorithms to identify critical temporal windows and potential non-linear decay patterns in the sensitivity-attachment link.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical map of how temporal design choices bias estimates in a core developmental model. It will offer methodological guidance for future longitudinal work and refine theoretical models by identifying developmentally sensitive periods where susceptibility traits most strongly organize attachment outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While timing is acknowledged as important, no pre-2026 study has systematically quantified its effect as a central research question. This proposal uniquely frames measurement interval not as a confound but as the primary variable of interest, applying advanced modeling to a synthesized dataset to uncover fundamental temporal dynamics.

proposals.title: The Sensitivity Network: A Psychometric Network Analysis of Environmental Sensitivity Components and Their Causal Links to Infant Attachment Security
proposals.motivation: The differential susceptibility and sensory processing sensitivity frameworks posit that some individuals are more sensitive to both positive and negative environments, influencing developmental outcomes like attachment. However, sensitivity is a multidimensional construct (e.g., perceptual sensitivity, emotional reactivity, behavioral responsivity) whose internal dynamics and specific pathways to attachment remain opaque. Pre-2026, research often treats these components as additive or uses latent variable models, which may obscure the causal interplay between specific sensitivity behaviors and attachment formation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that psychometric network analysis will reveal a core, stable cluster of sensitivity behaviors (e.g., orienting to subtle cues, depth of processing) that holds the highest centrality and predictive strength for attachment security, and that this network structure will differ between infants with high versus low genetic susceptibility markers (e.g., polygenic scores related to dopamine or serotonin systems).
proposals.method: We will apply psychometric network analysis (specifically Gaussian Graphical Models and Directed Acyclic Graphs) to intensive longitudinal observational data of caregiver-infant dyads (N=300) from the Generation R Study. Sensitivity components (e.g., from the Highly Sensitive Child scale and coded lab observations) and attachment security (Strange Situation Procedure) will be modeled as nodes. The EBICglasso algorithm will estimate the regularized partial correlation network to identify core nodes. The pc-algorithm will be used for exploratory causal discovery to suggest directional links between sensitivity components and attachment outcomes, controlling for temperament and caregiving quality.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond correlational and latent factor models to provide a dynamic, component-level map of sensitivity. It will identify which specific, observable sensitivity behaviors are most causally potent for attachment, offering precise targets for intervention. It will also test if the network structure itself is a function of genetic susceptibility, integrating molecular genetics with dynamic psychometrics.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis has been applied to psychopathology, its application to normative developmental constructs like sensitivity and attachment is novel. This approach reframes sensitivity not as a unitary trait but as a dynamic system, allowing for the first causal hypothesis-testing about which specific sensitivity components are mechanistic drivers of attachment security.

proposals.title: Genetic and Environmental Architecture of Environmental Sensitivity: A Twin Study of Differential Susceptibility to Positive and Negative Parenting
